ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn provides a general chicken breast food entity; the roasted/marinated preparation specific to this Singapore café dish is not individually coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked chicken meat broadly; no specific code exists for roasted marinated chicken breast ala carte. Closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 covers roast chicken generically; no specific ala carte breast-only code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical ala carte serving of roasted chicken breast in a Singapore café or Western restaurant is approximately 150 g, representing one whole chicken breast portion with marinade.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Chicken Breast — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010417 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with provenance marked canonical and confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 05064, roasted chicken breast without skin) at approximately 2.5 g/100g, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and water are set to -1 as HPB does not report these for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for roasted chicken breast with soy-honey marinade. GI is estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on the analogous food category of marinated/sauced chicken dishes with small amounts of added sugar and honey. The carbohydrate content is low at 7.34 g/100g, predominantly from marinade sugars. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (40 × 7.34 × 1.50) / 100 = 4.4 / 2 ≈ 2.2 (rounded), classified as low GL. Estimation basis: University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for chicken with sauce/marinade and peer-reviewed GI tables (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 454 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods, driven by soy sauce and salt in the marinade.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce in the marinade or substitute low-sodium soy sauce to reduce sodium by approximately 40%; potassium at 280 mg/100g provides partial offset but is insufficient to neutralise the sodium load at this level.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High protein at 21.15 g/100g supports blood glucose stability; carbohydrate is low at 7.34 g/100g with a low estimated GI of approximately 40.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted chicken breast is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate and high protein content; however, the 5.98 g/100g sugar from honey or sugar in the marinade should be minimised by requesting reduced sweetener, and pairing with high-fibre vegetables is recommended to moderate the overall glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 454 mg/100g is elevated for cardiovascular health; saturated fat is moderate at approximately 2.5 g/100g and total fat at 9.15 g/100g is acceptable for a lean protein source.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium marinade preparation, as the sodium content is the primary cardiovascular concern; the lean protein profile and absence of trans fat are favourable, and cholesterol at 78 mg/100g is within acceptable limits for most cardiovascular dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Roasting method avoids AGE formation from deep-frying; garlic and ginger in the marinade provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols (allicin, gingerols); saturated fat at approximately 2.5 g/100g is well below the 5 g threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Roasted chicken breast is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory marinade ingredients and lean preparation method; sodium at 454 mg/100g may worsen RA-related tissue swelling and reduced-soy-sauce preparation is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Chicken Breast Ala Carte is a Western-style dish served in Singapore cafés and restaurants, consisting of a single chicken breast portion marinated in a soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and honey or sugar blend, then oven-roasted until cooked through. The result is a tender, mildly savoury-sweet protein with a lightly caramelised exterior from the honey marinade. Per 100 g, the dish provides 202 kcal, 21.15 g protein, 9.15 g fat, and 7.34 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 454 mg driven by the soy sauce marinade. The high protein content makes it a strong choice for muscle maintenance and blood glucose stability. Fibre is very low at 0.2 g/100g, so pairing with high-fibre vegetables is recommended. Sodium is the primary clinical concern, exceeding the 400 mg/100g threshold relevant to hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is classified as minimally processed, contains soy as an allergen, and is suitable for halal diets. It is commonly ordered as a standalone protein component at lunch or dinner in Singapore Western-style food establishments.
common_user_queries: How many calories in roasted chicken breast ala carte?
Is roasted chicken breast suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat roasted chicken breast if I have high blood pressure?
How much protein in roasted chicken breast?
What is the sodium content of roasted chicken breast?
Is roasted chicken breast good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database (cr_id G00010417, provenance canonical, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat is supplemented from USDA FoodData Central as the closest approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, documented accordingly.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture data for this specific roasted chicken breast ala carte preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010417 as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat at 2.5 g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 05064 (roasted chicken breast without skin, adapted for marinade fat contribution) as the closest approved secondary source, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond the reported minerals is not available in the HPB record for this dish. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific marinated roasted chicken breast preparation was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is classified as Western cuisine served in Singapore; multilingual names are provided in English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements.
